coredata.dc:title: A study on lattice parameters of martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Changwei
Guo, Fenfang
Yang, Dazhi
coredata.dc:description: The lattice parameters of B19′ martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys are analyzed by means of the X-ray diffraction (XRD), the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the multiphase Rietveld methods. Experimental results indicate that when the Ni content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ta content; when the Ta content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ni content. With the unit cell volume V increased, the R factor and the aberration of unit cell increase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys
Martensitic transformation
Lattice parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DSC curves of Ni50Ti47Ta3, Ni50Ti45Ta5 and Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloys.
XRD patterns of the solution-treated Ni50Ti47Ta3, Ni50Ti45Ta5 and Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloys.
(a) TEM bright-field image of the solution-treated Ni50Ti45Ta5 alloy. SADP of (a) with: (b) [100]M zone axis and (c) [010]M zone axis.

Results of the Rietveld refinement for P21/m model. Observed diffraction profile and calculated one for (a) Ni50Ti47Ta3, (b) Ni50Ti45Ta5 and (c) Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloys, respectively. In each figure, the lower curve stands for the deviations between observed and calculated intensities.
Transformation temperatures for solution-treated Ni–Ti–Ta alloys
The lattice parameters and unit cell volume of B19′ martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta alloys
R factors for three Ni–Ti–Ta alloys

article.ce:title: A study on lattice parameters of martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Section of Measurement, Fenxi Machinery Plant, Taiyuan 030027, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 76023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The lattice parameters of B19′ martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys are analyzed by means of the X-ray diffraction (XRD), the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the multiphase Rietveld methods. Experimental results indicate that when the Ni content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ta content; when the Ta content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ni content. With the unit cell volume V increased, the R factor and the aberration of unit cell increase.

ce:section.ce:para: The Ni–Ti shape memory alloys (SMAs), as implanted materials, are currently a topic of notable interest in the medicine field because of unique shape memory effect (SME) and superelasticity (SE) [1–3]. The high nickel content of Ni–Ti could cause toxicity [4]; thus, more investigations are needed to use them widely in the medicine field. Recently, the Ni–Ti alloys have been used in the interventional operation. It is very difficult to detect the very small implantable devices or tools that are made of NiTi alloys due to low X-ray visibility. It is absolutely necessarily to improve the X-ray visibility.

The third element Ta addition can increase X-ray visibility [5,6]. The transformation temperatures of Ni–Ti–Ta alloys are less sensitive to the Ni content [5]. Due to these features, it is attractive to biomedical application for the Ni–Ti–Ta alloys. The phase transformation behaviors, the effects of thermo-mechanical treatments and precipitated second phase particles in Ni–Ti–Ta SMAs have been studied [7,8]. As we know, the third element addition can influence the mechanical properties of binary Ni–Ti alloy, which is relative to its crystal structures, but the lattice parameters of B19′ martensite changing with Ta content in these alloys have not yet been reported. In this study, the effects of Ta content on lattice parameters of martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta SMAs are investigated using the X-ray diffraction (XRD), the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the multiphase Rietveld methods.

The Ni50Ti47Ta3, Ni50Ti45Ta5 and Ni49Ti46Ta5 (at.%) alloys were melted in a high frequency vacuum induced furnace. To improve the metallurgy quality, the master alloy Ni50.8Ti49.2 (at.%) was used. At the same time, 99.8wt% Ta plate, 99.5wt% electrolytic Ni plate and 99.7wt% sponge Ti were used as raw materials. A pure titanium block was also used and melted before melting the alloys as an oxygen getter. The mass loss during the melting was negligibly small. The as-melted blocks were homogenized, forged and then hot drawn to 8mm in diameter. The rods were cut into several plates with a low speed diamond saw and annealed for 1h at 850°C and then quenched into water. The acquired specimens were polished for differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), XRD and TEM examinations.

The phase transformation temperatures were measured using a Mettler Toledo DSC822e DSC with a heating/cooling rate of 5K/min. The temperature range of measurements is from 0 to 80°C. X-ray diffraction measurements were performed in step scan mode with a step interval of 0.02° using a Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.114060nm) at room temperature. X-ray diffraction patterns were analyzed and refined by means of the multiphase Rietveld method [9]. In this paper, we did not refine atom-positional, thermal and site-occupancy parameters, and only refine the parameters for the background, lattice, instrumental geometrical–optical features, specimen aberrations and specimen reflection profile broadening agents such as crystallite size and microstrain. Multiple phases may also be refined simultaneously and comparative analysis of the separate overall scale factors for the phases offers what is probably the most reliable current method for quantitative phase analysis. Specimens for the TEM observation were prepared by jet electron polishing with a solution of one-part HNO3 and three-parts CH3OH at around −30°C. TEM observations were carried out on a Philips Tecnai G2 20 electron microscopy operated at 200kV, using FEI double-tilt specimen stage. The images were recorded using CCD slow-scan camera and then analyzed by the Digital Micrograph software.

Fig. 1 shows the experimental results of DSC measurements for the solution-treated Ni50Ti47Ta3, Ni50Ti45Ta5 and Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloys on both heating and cooling cycles. The peak temperatures M * and A * (including forward martensitic transformation start temperatures (M s), forward martensitic transformation finish temperatures (M f), reverse martensitic transformation start temperatures (A s) and reverse martensitic transformation finish temperatures (A f)) appearing in Fig. 1 are listed in Table 1 for the three alloys. It can be seen from Fig. 1 that the transformations of these alloys are characterized as one-stage B2↔B19′ transformation.

In the Ni–Ti–Ta alloys, a great number of second phase particles are seen around the grain boundary with (Ti, Ta)2Ni and β-Ta phases [7]. The β-Ta phase is a soft phase. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns for the three Ni–Ti–Ta alloys. The lattice parameters of the B19′ martensite are calculated by three methods: (1) comparing these results with those of the B19′ martensite in the Ni50Ti50 alloy [10], from the principal peaks of
( 1
1 ¯
0 )
M
, (002)M,
( 1
1
1 ¯
)
M
, (020)M, (111)M and (022)M shown in Fig. 2; (2) refining and analyzing the X-ray diffraction results by the multiphase Rietveld method; (3) analyzing the electron diffracting patterns. Table 2 lists the calculated lattice parameters a, b, c, β and the unit cell volume V of the B19′ martensite, where a, b and c are lattice constants and β is the monoclinic angle of the B19′ martensite. From Table 2, it can be seen when the Ni content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ta content; when the Ta content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ni content. It is found that the volume of β-Ta phase particles in Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloy is more than that of Ni50Ti45Ta5 alloy [7]. Thus, the amount of Ta atoms in the solid solution of the NiTi matrix of Ni50Ti45Ta5 alloy is more than that of Ni49Ti46Ta5 alloy. The Ta atom radius of Ta (0.149nm) is larger than Ti (0.147nm) and Ni (0.124nm). The change of lattice parameters is consistent with that of volume of β-Ta phase particles.

Fig. 3(a) shows the TEM bright field image of martensite in solution-treated Ni50Ti45Ta5 alloy. Fig. 3(b and c) is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern along [100]M and [010]M directions. From Fig. 3(b and c), one can calculate the lattice parameters and the results are shown in Table 2. Fig. 4(a–c) shows the results of the multiphase Rietveld analysis method of three solution-treated Ni–Ti–Ta alloys. In the upper curves of Fig. 4(a), the dashed lines indicate the observed XRD data of Fig. 2 and the solid lines indicate the pattern calculated by the multiphase Rietveld analysis method using the B19′ martensitic structure model (monoclinic, the space group P21/m [11]). The differences between the observed and calculated values are shown in the lower curves of Fig. 4(a). Fig. 4(a) indicates that the curve fitting of calculated peaks to observed ones, such as
( 1
1 ¯
0 )
M
, (002)M,
( 1
1
1 ¯
)
M
, (020)M, (111)M and (022)M of Fig. 2, is rather good. The final reliability, R factors, R p and R wp for the three Ni–Ti–Ta alloys, are listed in Table 3 . The subscripts ‘wp’ and ‘p’ mean weighed pattern and pattern, respectively. Because the atom-positional, thermal and site-occupancy parameters are not refined, the values of R p and R wp are rather high. From Table 3, it can be seen when the Ni content is constant, R factor increases with increasing Ta content; when the Ta content is constant, the R factor increases with increasing Ni content. The lower the R factor is, the better the curve fitting of calculated peaks to observed ones for the B19′ martensite. It is suggested that more Ta addition cause more aberration of unit cell for the B19′ martensite. A further study on the structure change for more Ta addition Ni–Ti–Ta alloy is being conducted now.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The phase transformations of Ni–Ti–Ta shape memory alloys are characterized as one-stage B2↔B19′ transformation.

The lattice parameters of martensite in Ni–Ti–Ta alloys are analyzed by means of the XRD, the SADPs of TEM and the Rietveld methods. The results show that when the Ni content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ta content; when the Ta content is constant, the lattice parameters a and c, monoclinic angle β and unit cell volume V increase, while lattice parameter b decreases with increasing Ni content. With the unit cell volume V increased, the R factor and the aberration of unit cell increase.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: C.W. Gong, Y.N. Wang, D.Z. Yang, J. Alloys Compd., in press.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pearson's Handbook of Crystallographic Data for Intermetallic Phases
